key_insight: Constrain adaptation to a trusted low-dimensional subspace learned from existing adapters, and you can preserve useful task learning while sharply limiting how much a poisoned objective can be expressed.
engineering_relevance: This offers a practical robustness mechanism for fine-tuning deployed models: keep adaptability for covered tasks, but reduce susceptibility to label inversion and backdoor poisoning when the adapter pool is trusted.
method: Estimate a shared subspace from 196 public LoRA adapters on flan-t5-large.
Restrict gradient-based adaptation to 128 coordinates in that subspace instead of full LoRA updates.
Compare clean fine-tuning, targeted label inversion, and adaptive backdoor attacks under the constrained and unconstrained settings.
evidence_signal: Across the evaluated tasks, the constrained learner matched full LoRA on clean data, retained 62-96% exact match under label inversion on covered tasks, and cut adaptation loss by about 120x between clean and corrupted data.
watch_item: The defense depends on the trustworthiness and coverage of the adapter pool, and it weakens when the desired target behavior is far from the pool's learned directions.
paper_title: Learning Only What Valid Adapters Can Express: Subspace-Constrained Adaptation Against Fine-Tuning Poisoning
